title: Conjecture 1.6 (Korándi, Pach, and Tomon)
statement_text: Let $H$ be an ordered bigraph such that both $H$ and its bicomplement are forests. Then there exists $\varepsilon > 0$ with the following property. Let $G$ be an ordered bigraph that does not contain $H$, with $|V_1(G)|, |V_2(G)| \geq n$; then there is a pure pair $(Z_1, Z_2)$ with $Z_i \subseteq V_i(G)$ and $|Z_i| \geq \varepsilon n$ for $i = 1, 2$.
context_text: Korándi, Pach, and Tomon observed that any ordered bigraph $H$ satisfying a linear pure-pair conclusion must itself be a forest whose bicomplement is also a forest, and conjectured this condition is also sufficient. They proved the conjecture for certain matrices with at most two rows. The present paper cannot resolve the full conjecture but proves $f_H(n) = n^{1-o(1)}$ for every acyclic matrix $H$ via Theorems 1.12 and 1.14.
paper_contribution: Proves that for every ordered tree bigraph $T$, there is a pure pair $(Z_1, Z_2)$ with $|Z_i| \geq n^{1-o(1)}$, establishing $f_H(n) = n^{1-o(1)}$ for every acyclic matrix $H$, which falls short of the conjectured linear bound but significantly strengthens the polynomial lower bound previously known.
paper_title: Pure pairs. VII. Homogeneous submatrices in 0/1-matrices with a forbidden submatrix

title: Conjecture 1.7 (Korándi, Pach, and Tomon)
statement_text: For every ordered forest bigraph $H$, there exists $\varepsilon > 0$ with the following property. Let $G$ be an ordered bigraph that does not contain $H$ or its bicomplement, with $|V_1(G)|, |V_2(G)| \geq n$: then there is a pure pair $(Z_1, Z_2)$ with $Z_i \subseteq V_i(G)$ and $|Z_i| \geq \varepsilon n$ for $i = 1, 2$.
context_text: Korándi, Pach, and Tomon proposed this stronger conjecture as a natural ordered-bigraph analogue of the unordered results 1.1 and 1.2; it implies Conjecture 1.6. The present paper notes the analogy but expresses doubt about its truth, and does not resolve it.
paper_contribution: Does not prove or disprove Conjecture 1.7; the paper's authors believe it is likely false, and suggest $n/\operatorname{polylog}(n)$ as a possible correct bound.
paper_title: Pure pairs. VII. Homogeneous submatrices in 0/1-matrices with a forbidden submatrix

title: Informal belief on Conjecture 1.7 and polylog bound
statement_text: Our guess is that Conjecture 1.7 is false. Perhaps $n/\operatorname{polylog}(n)$ might be true?
context_text: After stating Conjecture 1.7, the authors remark that while it is a natural extension of the unordered theory, they do not believe it holds, and informally suggest that a bound of $n/\operatorname{polylog}(n)$ for the sizes of the pure pair might be the correct weaker conclusion.
notes: Stated as an informal belief/guess in running prose; no labelled theorem environment.
paper_title: Pure pairs. VII. Homogeneous submatrices in 0/1-matrices with a forbidden submatrix
